skill.name: drizzle-patterns
skill.description: Reusable Drizzle ORM patterns for PostgreSQL schema definitions, relations, queries, mutations, transactions, and migrations.
skill.summary: Apply common Drizzle ORM and PostgreSQL patterns in TypeScript projects.
skill.author: Barnhardt-Enterprises-Inc

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged fenced Markdown code blocks as Ruby shell backticks, which are false positives because they are documentation fences in SKILL.md. The only meaningful concerns are documented Drizzle commands and DATABASE_URL examples that users may copy into projects; no malicious intent, exfiltration, network beaconing, or prompt injection was found.

low_findings.title: Documented Database Credential Environment Access
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE as documentation: SKILL.md shows process.env.DATABASE_URL in Drizzle client and configuration examples. This is normal for database tooling, but users should protect the value and avoid logging or committing environment files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The environment variable references are directly visible and are used as database connection strings. The context is legitimate Drizzle configuration, and there is no evidence of network exfiltration or secret harvesting.

low_findings.title: Database Migration Commands Require User Control
low_findings.description: TRUE_POSITIVE as documentation: SKILL.md includes pnpm drizzle-kit generate, push, and studio commands. These are legitimate Drizzle workflows, but push and studio can affect a configured database if users run them against the wrong environment.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and tied to normal Drizzle migration workflows. Risk is limited because the skill does not execute them automatically, but user-run commands can change database state.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick Execution Alerts Are Markdown Fences
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: the static analyzer flagged multiple fenced Markdown code blocks as Ruby or shell backtick execution. The affected lines delimit TypeScript and Bash examples, not executable skill logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged tokens are Markdown code fence delimiters around examples. There is no Ruby source, command substitution expression, or automatic execution path in the skill file.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Identifier False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: the weak cryptography alerts match ordinary text or Drizzle query helper identifiers, including description text and desc ordering examples. No cryptographic algorithm is implemented or recommended.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines contain descriptive metadata and Drizzle ordering helpers, not hashing or encryption code. No weak algorithm usage is semantically present.

dangerous_patterns.title: Database-Affecting CLI Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents Drizzle Kit commands, including a database push command. These commands are expected for the domain but should be run only against the intended database environment.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are present and can mutate database schema when a user runs them. They are not executed by the skill itself.

content.value_statement: Drizzle ORM setup can be repetitive and easy to implement inconsistently. This skill provides focused PostgreSQL patterns for schemas, relations, queries, mutations, transactions, migrations, and configuration.
actual_capabilities: Shows PostgreSQL table definitions with Drizzle column types and inferred TypeScript models.
Demonstrates one-to-many relation setup between users and posts.
Provides examples for findFirst, findMany, filters, ordering, and relational queries.
Covers insert, bulk insert, update, delete, and transaction patterns.
Includes Drizzle Kit migration commands and a PostgreSQL drizzle.config.ts example.
limitations: It is a pattern reference, not a complete application scaffold.
It does not cover every PostgreSQL column type or index option.
It assumes Node.js, TypeScript, Drizzle ORM, and PostgreSQL are already selected.
It does not validate a project-specific schema or migration history.

use_cases.title: Start a typed database layer
use_cases.description: Use the schema, client, and inferred model examples to create a consistent Drizzle setup for a PostgreSQL project.

use_cases.title: Add relational queries
use_cases.description: Adapt the relation and query examples when adding linked records such as users, posts, profiles, or settings.

use_cases.title: Standardize migration workflows
use_cases.description: Use the migration and configuration patterns to align local development steps across a TypeScript team.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic schema
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for one PostgreSQL table.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the drizzle-patterns skill to draft a Drizzle PostgreSQL schema for a users table with typed insert and select models.

prompt_templates.title: Add relations and queries
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the drizzle-patterns skill to model users and posts, then explain the relation and a query that returns users with posts.

prompt_templates.title: Plan write operations
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the drizzle-patterns skill to outline insert, update, delete, and transaction patterns for account creation.

prompt_templates.title: Review a database layer
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the drizzle-patterns skill to review my Drizzle PostgreSQL setup for schema consistency, relation usage, migration safety, and transaction boundaries.

output_examples.input: Ask for a Drizzle PostgreSQL user schema.
output_examples.output: A concise schema plan with UUID primary keys, email uniqueness, timestamps, and inferred TypeScript model guidance.

output_examples.input: Ask how to query users with posts.
output_examples.output: A relation-focused explanation that describes the user-to-post link, filtering options, ordering, limits, and when to include related records.
A short operational checklist covering generation, review, database target confirmation, push timing, and local Studio use.

best_practices: Keep schema definitions, relations, and migration configuration organized in predictable database modules.
Review generated migrations before applying them to shared or production databases.
Store DATABASE_URL in protected environment configuration and avoid committing secret values.
anti_patterns: Do not run database push commands against production without an explicit release process.
Do not hardcode connection strings or credentials in schema and configuration files.
Do not skip transactions when several writes must succeed or fail together.

faq.question: Does this skill generate a full Drizzle project?
faq.answer: No. It provides reusable patterns that can be adapted inside an existing TypeScript and PostgreSQL project.

faq.question: Which database does it target?
faq.answer: It targets PostgreSQL with Drizzle ORM and Drizzle Kit.

faq.question: Can it help with relations?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for defining one-to-many relations and querying related records.

faq.question: Does it execute migrations automatically?
faq.answer: No. It documents Drizzle Kit commands. Users decide when and where to run them.

faq.question: Does it include security guidance?
faq.answer: It shows environment-based database configuration. Users must still protect credentials and review migrations.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, or Claude Code use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
